hypotheses.title: Detection of AI-Assisted FortiGate Compromise Campaign
hypotheses.description: A Russian-speaking threat actor is using commercial generative AI tools to automate the scanning and exploitation of exposed FortiGate firewalls via weak credentials and exposed management ports. This hypothesis hunts for lateral movement and command execution originating from compromised network edge devices that may be acting as pivot points.
mitre_attack_techniques: T1021.001 - Remote Services: Remote Desktop Protocol
T1059.001 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell

data_sources_needed.specific_data: Firewall logs, NetFlow, and IDS/IPS alerts for FortiGate management interfaces (port 443, 8443, 8080)
EDR logs for PowerShell execution, Go/Python recon tools, and lateral movement attempts

hunting_tactics.description: Identify successful logins to FortiGate management interfaces from unusual internal or external IPs, followed by configuration changes or outbound connections.
sample_queries: Splunk: index=firewall sourcetype=fortinet:traffic action=login status=success dest_port=443 | stats count by src_ip, user | where count > 5
KQL: DeviceLog | where DeviceVendor == "Fortinet" and EventID == "1000" and Result == "Success" | summarize count() by SourceIP, AccountName | where count_ > 3
hunting_tactics.look_for: Multiple failed logins followed by a success, or logins from IPs not in the trusted admin range, followed by outbound connections to unknown C2.

hunting_tactics.description: Look for automated scanning patterns or the execution of Go/Python scripts commonly used in AI-assisted reconnaissance campaigns.
sample_queries: Splunk: index=endpoint ProcessName="go.exe" OR ProcessName="python.exe" CommandLine="*scan*" OR CommandLine="*recon*" | stats count by ProcessName, CommandLine
KQL: DeviceProcessEvents | where FileName in~ ("go.exe", "python.exe") and CommandLine contains "scan" | project Timestamp, DeviceName, FileName, CommandLine
hunting_tactics.look_for: Execution of Go or Python binaries with arguments suggesting network scanning or credential testing.

network_patterns: Outbound connections from FortiGate to non-standard ports
High volume of failed SSH/HTTPS logins to port 443/8443
other_indicators: Use of AI-generated scripts for scanning
Rapid succession of port scans from a single host

recommended_actions: Immediately isolate compromised FortiGate devices from the network.
Reset all administrative credentials for network devices.
Block outbound traffic from network devices to unknown external IPs.
Review firewall rules for unauthorized changes.

hypotheses.title: Supply Chain Compromise via Malicious NPM/Go Packages (SANDWORM_MODE & Cline)
hypotheses.description: Attackers are injecting malicious code into popular developer tools (Cline CLI, npm packages) to steal API keys, CI secrets, and cryptocurrency wallets. This hypothesis targets the execution of malicious payloads within developer environments and CI/CD pipelines.
mitre_attack_techniques: T1195.002 - Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain
T1003.001 - OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory

data_sources_needed.specific_data: Process creation logs, file integrity monitoring for node_modules, and network connections from developer tools
CI/CD pipeline logs, API key usage logs, and container registry activity

hunting_tactics.description: Detect the execution of known malicious packages or unexpected network calls from developer tools like Cline, npm, or VS Code extensions.
sample_queries: Splunk: index=endpoint ProcessName="node.exe" CommandLine="*Cline*" OR CommandLine="*npm*" | search "OpenClaw" OR "SANDWORM" OR "Atomic"
KQL: DeviceProcessEvents | where FileName == "node.exe" and CommandLine contains "Cline" | where CommandLine contains "install" | project Timestamp, DeviceName, CommandLine
hunting_tactics.look_for: Execution of 'Cline' or 'npm install' followed by network connections to suspicious domains or the installation of 'OpenClaw' or 'Atomic' components.

hunting_tactics.description: Identify attempts to read environment variables or access CI/CD secrets from within a compromised build environment.
sample_queries: Splunk: index=ci_cd sourcetype=jenkins OR sourcetype=github_actions | search "GITHUB_TOKEN" OR "AWS_SECRET" OR "npm_token" | stats count by job_name, user
KQL: CloudAuditLogs | where OperationName contains "ReadSecret" or "GetToken" | where ResourceGroup contains "CI" or "CD" | project Timestamp, Caller, ResourceId
hunting_tactics.look_for: Unusual access to secrets, API keys, or tokens from build agents, especially if the agent is running unexpected code.

network_patterns: Outbound HTTPS from developer tools to non-standard C2
Connections to Pastebin for dead drops
other_indicators: Execution of 'go' or 'python' scripts within node_modules

recommended_actions: Revoke all CI/CD API keys and tokens immediately.
Audit all installed npm packages and VS Code extensions for known malicious versions.
Isolate developer workstations showing signs of infection.
Scan repositories for injected malicious code or backdoors.

hypotheses.title: OAuth Redirect Abuse for Malware Delivery and Credential Theft
hypotheses.description: Attackers are abusing legitimate OAuth error redirection mechanisms in Microsoft Entra ID and Google Workspace to bypass phishing filters and deliver malware or steal credentials. This hypothesis hunts for suspicious OAuth flows that redirect to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
mitre_attack_techniques: T1059.001 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell
T1071.001 - Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols

data_sources_needed.specific_data: OAuth consent logs, sign-in logs, and conditional access policies
Email headers, URL click logs, and phishing detection logs

hunting_tactics.description: Identify OAuth authorization requests that result in redirects to non-standard or suspicious domains, particularly those using error parameters.
sample_queries: Splunk: index=identity sourcetype=azure_ad_signin | search "error=access_denied" OR "redirect_uri" | stats count by redirect_uri, user_principal_name | where count > 1
KQL: SigninLogs | where ResultType != 0 | where AppDisplayName in~ ("Microsoft Entra ID", "Google Workspace") | summarize count() by RedirectUri, UserPrincipalName | where count_ > 2
hunting_tactics.look_for: OAuth flows where the redirect_uri points to a domain not whitelisted for the application, or where the flow involves an error state that triggers a redirect.

hunting_tactics.description: Analyze clicked links in emails that appear to be legitimate OAuth providers but redirect to malicious sites.
sample_queries: Splunk: index=email sourcetype=click_tracking | search "login.microsoftonline.com" OR "accounts.google.com" | search "redirect" OR "error" | stats count by destination_url, sender
KQL: EmailUrlClickEvents | where Url contains "login.microsoftonline.com" or Url contains "accounts.google.com" | where Url contains "redirect" | project Timestamp, User, Url
hunting_tactics.look_for: Links that start with a legitimate domain but contain parameters that redirect to a malicious domain (e.g., `?redirect=https://malicious-site.com`).

network_patterns: HTTP 302 redirects from legitimate OAuth domains to suspicious IPs
OAuth token requests with unusual scopes
other_indicators: Presence of 'redirect_uri' parameters in OAuth requests pointing to non-whitelisted domains
High volume of OAuth consent requests from a single user

recommended_actions: Block the identified malicious redirect domains at the proxy/firewall level.
Review and restrict OAuth consent policies to only allow whitelisted applications.
Force password resets for users who clicked suspicious OAuth links.
Enable Conditional Access policies to block sign-ins from suspicious locations.

hypotheses.title: Exploitation of BeyondTrust CVE-2026-1731 for Ransomware Deployment
hypotheses.description: Threat actors are actively exploiting a critical RCE vulnerability (CVE-2026-1731) in BeyondTrust Remote Support and Privileged Remote Access products to deploy VShell and SparkRAT, leading to ransomware attacks. This hypothesis focuses on detecting unauthorized access and lateral movement via compromised PAM tools.
mitre_attack_techniques: T1059.001 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell
T1021.002 - Remote Services: SMB/Windows Admin Shares

data_sources_needed.specific_data: EDR logs for VShell, SparkRAT, and PowerShell execution on PAM servers
Firewall logs for connections to BeyondTrust management interfaces

hunting_tactics.description: Monitor for unauthorized access attempts and successful exploitation of BeyondTrust management consoles.
sample_queries: Splunk: index=endpoint ProcessName="VShell.exe" OR ProcessName="SparkRAT.exe" | stats count by ProcessName, DeviceName
KQL: DeviceProcessEvents | where FileName in~ ("VShell.exe", "SparkRAT.exe") | project Timestamp, DeviceName, FileName, CommandLine
hunting_tactics.look_for: Execution of VShell or SparkRAT binaries, or PowerShell commands that interact with BeyondTrust APIs.

hunting_tactics.description: Detect lateral movement originating from compromised PAM servers using stolen credentials.
sample_queries: Splunk: index=identity sourcetype=windows_security | search EventID=4624 LogonType=10 | search "BeyondTrust" OR "PAM" | stats count by TargetUser, SourceIP
KQL: SigninLogs | where AppDisplayName contains "BeyondTrust" | where ResultType != 0 | summarize count() by UserPrincipalName, IPAddress
hunting_tactics.look_for: Successful logins to PAM systems from unusual IPs, followed by lateral movement to other critical systems.

network_patterns: Unusual traffic to BeyondTrust management ports (443, 8443)
Outbound connections from PAM servers to C2

recommended_actions: Immediately patch BeyondTrust products to the latest version.
Isolate any systems showing signs of VShell or SparkRAT execution.
Reset all credentials for PAM systems and administrative accounts.
Block outbound traffic from PAM servers to unknown destinations.

hypotheses.title: AI-Driven Android Malware (PromptSpy) Persistence and Evasion
hypotheses.description: A new Android malware, PromptSpy, uses Google's Gemini AI at runtime to analyze on-screen elements and maintain persistence by pinning itself in the recent apps list. This hypothesis hunts for mobile devices exhibiting AI-driven evasion behaviors and unauthorized access to sensitive data.
mitre_attack_techniques: T1543.003 - Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

data_sources_needed.specific_data: Mobile device logs, app installation logs, and network traffic from mobile devices
DNS logs and HTTPS traffic from mobile devices to AI APIs

hunting_tactics.description: Identify mobile apps that are using AI APIs to manipulate the user interface or maintain persistence.
sample_queries: Splunk: index=mobile sourcetype=android_log | search "Gemini" OR "AI" OR "persistence" | stats count by app_name, device_id
KQL: DeviceLog | where DeviceVendor == "Android" and Message contains "Gemini" | summarize count() by AppName, DeviceName
hunting_tactics.look_for: Apps making calls to Gemini AI APIs to analyze screen content or manage app states.

hunting_tactics.description: Detect mobile apps exfiltrating sensitive data like lockscreen data, screenshots, or device info.
sample_queries: Splunk: index=mobile sourcetype=network_traffic | search "screenshot" OR "lockscreen" OR "device_info" | stats count by destination_ip, app_name
KQL: NetworkFlow | where DestinationPort == 443 and (Url contains "screenshot" or Url contains "lockscreen") | project Timestamp, SourceIP, DestinationIP, Url
hunting_tactics.look_for: Outbound traffic containing keywords related to sensitive data exfiltration from mobile devices.

network_patterns: HTTPS traffic to AI APIs from unknown apps
Unusual data exfiltration patterns from mobile devices
other_indicators: Apps requesting excessive permissions (screen capture, accessibility)
AI API calls from non-standard apps

recommended_actions: Remove the PromptSpy app from infected devices immediately.
Revoke access tokens and reset passwords for accounts accessed on infected devices.
Update mobile security policies to restrict AI API access for non-approved apps.
Scan all mobile devices for similar AI-driven malware.

T1195.002 - Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain
